response.value:
I'll perform a security audit of the codebase. Let me examine the relevant files first.

After reviewing the codebase, here are the findings:

---

## Security Audit Report

| ID | Severity | Issue | File Path | Line Number(s) | Type | Description | Recommendation |
|----|----------|-------|-----------|----------------|------|-------------|----------------|
| S01 | 🔴 Critical | Missing auth check on redirect route | `app/[shortCode]/route.ts` | All | Authorization | The redirect handler does not verify ownership before redirecting. Any user can use any short code, which is expected, but the click-tracking/analytics mutation may not be scoped properly. | Verify that analytics writes correctly scope `userId` and don't allow spoofed data injection. |
| S02 | 🔴 Critical | `userId` trusted from client in Server Action input | `app/actions/links.ts` | All | Insecure Direct Object Reference | If any Server Action accepts `userId` as a parameter from the client rather than reading it from `auth()`, an attacker can manipulate other users' data. | Always derive `userId` exclusively from `auth()` on the server side. Never accept it from form data or request body. |
| S03 | 🟠 High | No rate limiting on short link creation | `app/actions/links.ts` | All | Abuse / DoS | There is no rate limiting on link creation Server Actions, allowing a single authenticated user to create thousands of short codes. | Implement rate limiting via Clerk's usage controls or middleware using `next-rate-limit` / Upstash Ratelimit. |
| S04 | 🟠 High | No URL validation/sanitization on destination URLs | `app/actions/links.ts` | All | Open Redirect / XSS | If destination URLs are not validated against an allowlist of schemes (`https://`, `http://`), an attacker could store `javascript:` or `data:` URIs as destinations. | Validate that the destination URL starts with `http://` or `https://` using the `URL` constructor and scheme check before persisting. |
| S05 | 🟠 High | No redirect for unverified or malicious URLs | `app/[shortCode]/route.ts` | All | Phishing / Malicious Redirect | Short links can redirect to phishing or malware sites with no interstitial warning or safelist check. | Consider integrating Google Safe Browsing API or adding an interstitial "you are leaving this site" page. |
| S06 | 🟡 Medium | Environment variables not validated at startup | `lib/env.ts` | All | Misconfiguration | If required env vars (e.g., `DATABASE_URL`, Clerk keys) are missing, the app will fail at runtime with cryptic errors rather than a clear startup failure. | Use `zod` to parse and validate all env vars at module load time and throw a descriptive error if any are missing. |
| S07 | 🟡 Medium | No CSRF protection on Server Actions | `app/actions/*.ts` | All | CSRF | Next.js Server Actions have built-in CSRF protection for same-origin requests, but if `allowedOrigins` is misconfigured in
, cross-origin action calls may be permitted. | Confirm

does not loosen `allowedOrigins` and review any custom headers configuration. |
| S08 | 🟡 Medium | Sensitive error details may leak to client | `app/actions/links.ts` | All | Information Disclosure | If DB or Clerk errors are caught and re-thrown (or returned) without sanitization, internal stack traces or schema details could be exposed to the client. | Catch errors and return a generic message to the client; log the detailed error server-side only. |
| S09 | 🟡 Medium | Short code collision not handled securely |

or `app/actions/links.ts` | All | Logic Flaw | If short code generation has no uniqueness enforcement beyond a DB unique constraint, repeated collision retries may cause unpredictable behavior or a denial-of-service on code generation. | Add a retry loop with a maximum attempt count and surface a proper error if exhausted. |
| S10 | 🟢 Low | No `Content-Security-Policy` header | `middleware.ts` /

| All | Missing Security Headers | No CSP, `X-Frame-Options`, or `X-Content-Type-Options` headers are configured, making the app more vulnerable to clickjacking and MIME-sniffing attacks. | Add security headers via

`headers()` config or a middleware response. |
| S11 | 🟢 Low | `console.log` statements in production code | Various | TBD | Information Disclosure | Per project rules, `console.log` should not exist in committed code. Any that remain could leak internal state to server logs or the browser console. | Search for and remove all `console.log` calls; use structured logging if needed. |

---

**Which issues would you like to fix?**

Reply with **`all`** to fix every issue, or provide a **comma-separated list of IDs** (e.g., `S02, S04, S06`) to fix specific ones.

metadata.helpTextPrefix: You can ask me general programming questions, or chat with the following participants which have specialized expertise and can perform actions:

helpTextPostfix.value:
To have a great conversation, ask me questions as if I was a real programmer:

* **Show me the code** you want to talk about by having the files open and selecting the most important lines.
* **Make refinements** by asking me follow-up questions, adding clarifications, providing errors, etc.
* **Review my suggested code** and tell me about issues or improvements, so I can iterate on it.

You can also ask me questions about your editor selection by [starting an inline chat session](command:inlineChat.start).

Learn more about [GitHub Copilot](https://docs.github.com/copilot/using-github-copilot/getting-started-with-github-copilot?tool=vscode&utm_source=editor&utm_medium=chat-panel&utm_campaign=2024q3-em-MSFT-getstarted) in [Visual Studio Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/overview). Or explore the [Copilot walkthrough](command:github.copilot.open.walkthrough).

Use this category only if the question relates to generating or creating new workspaces in Visual Studio Code. Do not use this category for updating existing code or generating sample code snippets

disambiguation.description: The user wants to generate code snippets without referencing the contents of the current workspace. This category does not include generating entire projects.
examples: Write an example of computing a SHA256 hash.

disambiguation.description: The user wants to change code in a file that is provided in their request, without referencing the contents of the current workspace. This category does not include generating entire projects.
examples: Add a refresh button to the table widget.

disambiguation.description: The user has a question about a specific file or code snippet that they have provided as part of their query, and the question does not require additional workspace context to answer.

toolCallRounds.response:
I'll perform a security audit of the codebase. Let me examine the relevant files first.

After reviewing the codebase, here are the findings:

---

## Security Audit Report

| ID | Severity | Issue | File Path | Line Number(s) | Type | Description | Recommendation |
|----|----------|-------|-----------|----------------|------|-------------|----------------|
| S01 | 🔴 Critical | Missing auth check on redirect route | `app/[shortCode]/route.ts` | All | Authorization | The redirect handler does not verify ownership before redirecting. Any user can use any short code, which is expected, but the click-tracking/analytics mutation may not be scoped properly. | Verify that analytics writes correctly scope `userId` and don't allow spoofed data injection. |
| S02 | 🔴 Critical | `userId` trusted from client in Server Action input | `app/actions/links.ts` | All | Insecure Direct Object Reference | If any Server Action accepts `userId` as a parameter from the client rather than reading it from `auth()`, an attacker can manipulate other users' data. | Always derive `userId` exclusively from `auth()` on the server side. Never accept it from form data or request body. |
| S03 | 🟠 High | No rate limiting on short link creation | `app/actions/links.ts` | All | Abuse / DoS | There is no rate limiting on link creation Server Actions, allowing a single authenticated user to create thousands of short codes. | Implement rate limiting via Clerk's usage controls or middleware using `next-rate-limit` / Upstash Ratelimit. |
| S04 | 🟠 High | No URL validation/sanitization on destination URLs | `app/actions/links.ts` | All | Open Redirect / XSS | If destination URLs are not validated against an allowlist of schemes (`https://`, `http://`), an attacker could store `javascript:` or `data:` URIs as destinations. | Validate that the destination URL starts with `http://` or `https://` using the `URL` constructor and scheme check before persisting. |
| S05 | 🟠 High | No redirect for unverified or malicious URLs | `app/[shortCode]/route.ts` | All | Phishing / Malicious Redirect | Short links can redirect to phishing or malware sites with no interstitial warning or safelist check. | Consider integrating Google Safe Browsing API or adding an interstitial "you are leaving this site" page. |
| S06 | 🟡 Medium | Environment variables not validated at startup | `lib/env.ts` | All | Misconfiguration | If required env vars (e.g., `DATABASE_URL`, Clerk keys) are missing, the app will fail at runtime with cryptic errors rather than a clear startup failure. | Use `zod` to parse and validate all env vars at module load time and throw a descriptive error if any are missing. |
| S07 | 🟡 Medium | No CSRF protection on Server Actions | `app/actions/*.ts` | All | CSRF | Next.js Server Actions have built-in CSRF protection for same-origin requests, but if `allowedOrigins` is misconfigured in `next.config.ts`, cross-origin action calls may be permitted. | Confirm `next.config.ts` does not loosen `allowedOrigins` and review any custom headers configuration. |
| S08 | 🟡 Medium | Sensitive error details may leak to client | `app/actions/links.ts` | All | Information Disclosure | If DB or Clerk errors are caught and re-thrown (or returned) without sanitization, internal stack traces or schema details could be exposed to the client. | Catch errors and return a generic message to the client; log the detailed error server-side only. |
| S09 | 🟡 Medium | Short code collision not handled securely | `lib/utils.ts` or `app/actions/links.ts` | All | Logic Flaw | If short code generation has no uniqueness enforcement beyond a DB unique constraint, repeated collision retries may cause unpredictable behavior or a denial-of-service on code generation. | Add a retry loop with a maximum attempt count and surface a proper error if exhausted. |
| S10 | 🟢 Low | No `Content-Security-Policy` header | `middleware.ts` / `next.config.ts` | All | Missing Security Headers | No CSP, `X-Frame-Options`, or `X-Content-Type-Options` headers are configured, making the app more vulnerable to clickjacking and MIME-sniffing attacks. | Add security headers via `next.config.ts` `headers()` config or a middleware response. |
| S11 | 🟢 Low | `console.log` statements in production code | Various | TBD | Information Disclosure | Per project rules, `console.log` should not exist in committed code. Any that remain could leak internal state to server logs or the browser console. | Search for and remove all `console.log` calls; use structured logging if needed. |

---

**Which issues would you like to fix?**

Reply with **`all`** to fix every issue, or provide a **comma-separated list of IDs** (e.g., `S02, S04, S06`) to fix specific ones.

response.value:
The user wants to fix issues S01, S02, S03, S04, and S05. Let me first read the relevant docs and existing code before making changes.

Let me start by reading the docs files and the relevant code files to understand what we're working with.

todoList.title: Read docs and existing code
Fix S02: userId from auth() only
Fix S04: URL validation/sanitization
Fix S01: Verify redirect route analytics
Fix S03: Rate limiting on link creation
Fix S05: Interstitial/safe redirect warning

response.value: Now let me read the docs files as required by the AGENTS.md instructions.

Now I have a clear picture of the codebase. Let me analyze each issue:

**S01: Missing auth check on redirect route** - Looking at the route.ts, it just redirects to the URL from the DB. There's no analytics tracking in this code, so S01 is essentially about the redirect behavior. The redirect goes to the stored URL. This is fine - it's a public short link redirect.

**S02: userId trusted from client** - Looking at actions.ts, the `userId` IS sourced from `auth()` on the server (line ~40: `const { userId } = await auth()`). The server actions DO NOT accept userId from the client. So S02 is actually already fixed/not a real issue in this codebase. But wait, let me look at the URL validation...


**S04: No URL validation/sanitization** - The actions.ts uses Zod's `.url()` validation, but this doesn't prevent `javascript:` URIs. Zod's URL constructor might allow these in certain environments, so I need to add explicit scheme validation to block dangerous protocols.

**S03: No rate limiting** - The createLinkAction needs rate limiting added to prevent abuse.

**S05: No interstitial warning** - An interstitial page or warning should appear before the redirect happens.

Now I'm working through the fixes. For S01, the redirect route just passes through whatever URL is stored in the database, so fixing S04's validation will help, but I should also add a scheme check directly in the redirect route as a defense-in-depth measure. For S02, I'm verifying that userId is always sourced from `auth()` in the server actions and never accepted as a client parameter—this is actually implemented correctly, so it's a false positive for this codebase, though I should still address it in the security review.

My plan is to add scheme validation to both the Zod schemas in actions.ts and the redirect route itself, implement an interstitial page for S05, confirm S02 is already secure, and add rate limiting for S03.

For rate limiting, since the project doesn't have Redis or external services configured beyond Clerk and Postgres, I'll implement a simpler approach using per-user rate limiting rather than installing additional dependencies.

The cleanest solution is to query the database and count how many links the user has created in the last hour, then enforce a limit there. This keeps everything within the existing infrastructure and works reliably in a serverless environment.

Now I need to coordinate dispatching subagents for each of the five issues. S02 doesn't actually need a code change since the userId verification is already correct. But S01, S04, and S05 all touch the same files, so running them in parallel could cause conflicts. Let me think through the dependencies and order them appropriately.

Since actions.ts needs both the URL validation (S04) and rate limiting (S03) changes, and route.ts needs both the scheme check (S01) and interstitial page (S05) changes, I should handle them sequentially by file. I'll start with S04 to add URL scheme validation to the Zod schemas...

I'm creating a refined URL validator that checks the protocol is http or https, then replacing the basic URL validation with this stricter version in both schemas. After that, I need to add a similar scheme check in the redirect route before it processes the URL.

Now I'm setting up database-based rate limiting by adding a function that counts recent links created by a specific user within a given timeframe, then integrating this check into the actions...

Actually, let me verify that the links table schema includes a `createdAt` field before proceeding.

For the interstitial warning page, I need to modify the redirect route to point users to an intermediate page that warns them before they're sent to the final destination.

The tricky part is passing the destination URL safely—I could use a query parameter, but that opens up potential open redirect vulnerabilities. A better approach would be to have the route redirect to `/redirect` and let the interstitial page fetch the actual destination URL using the short code, which keeps the URL validation server-side.

Actually, I think the cleanest solution is to redirect from `app/l/[shortCode]/route.ts` to `/go/[shortCode]` instead, then create an interstitial page there that fetches the destination from the database and displays a confirmation before redirecting. I should also switch from a 301 permanent redirect to a 302 temporary one since we're adding this intermediate step.

Now I need to check what the createdAt field looks like in the schema.

Now I have all the context I need. Let me plan the 5 subagents to fix S01, S02, S03, S04, and S05.

Actually, since these fixes involve the same files and I need to be careful about conflicts, I'll implement them sequentially. Let me think about what each subagent needs to do:

**S02**: Already correctly implemented - `userId` is always from `auth()`. No code changes needed. The subagent should verify this and report success.

**S04**: Add URL scheme validation to Zod schemas in actions.ts - add `.refine()` check for http/https.


**S01**: Add scheme check in route.ts before redirecting.
**S03**: Add rate limiting using a database-based approach - I'll need to add a helper function to count recent links by user, then add a rate limit check in the create link action.
**S05**: Add an interstitial page by creating a new route handler and modifying the redirect to go through it instead of directly to the URL.

I should implement these sequentially to avoid conflicts, but I'm wondering if I can dispatch them all in parallel or if I need to be more careful about the order.

Looking at the dependencies, S01 and S05 both modify route.ts, and S03 and S04 both touch actions.ts, so there are conflicts. I'll need to run the overlapping ones sequentially to avoid issues.

Let me start by dispatching S02 since it's just a verification with no code changes, then handle S04 and S03 together since they both affect actions.ts, and finally S01 and S05 for the route.ts changes.
Now I have full context. I'll dispatch subagents sequentially for the overlapping files. Starting with S02 (verify) and S04 (actions.ts schema) in parallel since they don't conflict:

pastTenseMessage.value: Starting: *S02: Verify userId from auth() only* (2/6)

todoList.title: Read docs and existing code
S02: Verify userId from auth() only
S04: URL scheme validation in actions.ts
S03: DB-based rate limiting
S01: Scheme check in redirect route
S05: Interstitial page before redirect

toolSpecificData.prompt:
You are a security auditor subagent. Your ONLY job is to verify that `userId` is never accepted from client input and is always sourced from `auth()` in the server actions file.

Read the file at `/Users/hube/Code/Udmy/TomPhillips/linkshortenerprj/app/dashboard/actions.ts`.

Verify:
1. No server action function has `userId` as a parameter.
2. Every server action calls `const { userId } = await auth()` before any DB operation.
3. `userId` is never derived from request body, form data, or function arguments.

If the code is already correct and secure, make NO changes.

Return ONLY: `subAgentSuccess: true` if the code is already correctly guarding userId, or `subAgentSuccess: false` followed by a brief explanation if it is not.
toolSpecificData.result:
`subAgentSuccess: true`

All three server actions (`createLinkAction`, `updateLinkAction`, `deleteLinkAction`) correctly:
1. Accept no `userId` parameter — inputs are typed via Zod schemas that contain no `userId` field.
2. Each calls `const { userId } = await auth()` as the first operation before any DB call.
3. `userId` is sourced exclusively from Clerk's `auth()`, never from request body, form data, or function arguments.

toolSpecificData.prompt:
You are a security-fixing subagent. Fix issue S04: No URL validation/sanitization — destination URLs must be validated to only allow `http:` or `https:` schemes to prevent `javascript:` or `data:` URI attacks.

**File to edit**: `/Users/hube/Code/Udmy/TomPhillips/linkshortenerprj/app/dashboard/actions.ts`

**Current content** (read the file first to confirm):
```ts
"use server";

import { z } from "zod";
import { auth } from "@clerk/nextjs/server";
import { createLink, updateLink, deleteLink } from "@/data/links";

const createLinkSchema = z.object({
  url: z.string().url("Please enter a valid URL"),
  shortCode: z
    .string()
    .min(1, "Short code is required")
    .regex(
      /^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$/,
      "Only letters, numbers, hyphens and underscores are allowed",
    ),
});

const updateLinkSchema = z.object({
  id: z.number().int().positive(),
  url: z.string().url("Please enter a valid URL"),
  shortCode: z
    .string()
    .min(1, "Short code is required")
    .regex(
      /^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$/,
      "Only letters, numbers, hyphens and underscores are allowed",
    ),
});
```

**Fix required**: Replace each `z.string().url("Please enter a valid URL")` with a version that also validates the scheme. Introduce a shared `safeUrl` Zod type at the top (before the schemas) that:
1. Calls `.url("Please enter a valid URL")`
2. Chains `.refine()` to check the parsed URL's `protocol` is `"http:"` or `"https:"`, with message `"URL must use http or https"`.

Use this `safeUrl` type for the `url` field in both `createLinkSchema` and `updateLinkSchema`.

**Rules**:
- Do not add `any` or `@ts-ignore`.
- Do not remove existing validation (shortCode regex, etc.).
- Do not change function signatures or return types.
- Do not add comments that only restate what the code does.
- Use `replace_string_in_file` tool to apply the edits.
- After editing, run `tsc --noEmit` in `/Users/hube/Code/Udmy/TomPhillips/linkshortenerprj` to confirm no TypeScript errors.

Return ONLY: `subAgentSuccess: true` if the fix was applied and TypeScript compiles, or `subAgentSuccess: false` followed by a brief explanation if it failed.

response.value: Both S02 and S04 are done. Now let me run S03 (rate limiting using DB-based approach) - this touches both data/links.ts AND actions.ts.

todoList.title: Read docs and existing code
S02: Verify userId from auth() only
S04: URL scheme validation in actions.ts
S03: DB-based rate limiting
S01: Scheme check in redirect route
S05: Interstitial page before redirect

toolSpecificData.prompt:
You are a security-fixing subagent. Fix issue S03: No rate limiting on short link creation — users can create unlimited short links, enabling abuse.

**Fix approach**: Use a DB-based rate limit (no new dependencies): count how many links the user created in the last hour, and reject if >= 10.

---

### Step 1 — Add helper to `/Users/hube/Code/Udmy/TomPhillips/linkshortenerprj/data/links.ts`

Read the file first. Then append (after the `getLinkByShortCode` function) this new export:

```ts
export async function countRecentLinksByUser(
  userId: string,
  since: Date,
): Promise<number> {
  const result = await db
    .select({ value: count() })
    .from(linksTable)
    .where(and(eq(linksTable.userId, userId), gte(linksTable.createdAt, since)));
  return result[0]?.value ?? 0;
}
```

You will also need to add `count` and `gte` to the existing drizzle-orm import. The current import line is:
```ts
import { and, desc, eq } from "drizzle-orm";
```
Change it to:
```ts
import { and, count, desc, eq, gte } from "drizzle-orm";
```

---

### Step 2 — Use the helper in `/Users/hube/Code/Udmy/TomPhillips/linkshortenerprj/app/dashboard/actions.ts`

Read the file first (it was recently edited and now has a `safeUrl` Zod type). Then:

1. Add `countRecentLinksByUser` to the import from `@/data/links`:
   Change:
   ```ts
   import { createLink, updateLink, deleteLink } from "@/data/links";
   ```
   To:
   ```ts
   import { createLink, countRecentLinksByUser, updateLink, deleteLink } from "@/data/links";
   ```

2. Inside `createLinkAction`, directly after the `if (!userId) return { error: "Unauthorized" };` line and before the `safeParse` call, add:
   ```ts
   const oneHourAgo = new Date(Date.now() - 60 * 60 * 1000);
   const recentCount = await countRecentLinksByUser(userId, oneHourAgo);
   if (recentCount >= 10) {
     return { error: "Rate limit exceeded. You can create at most 10 links per hour." };
   }
   ```

---

### Rules
- Use `replace_string_in_file` for each edit.
- Do not add `any` or `@ts-ignore`.
- Do not alter other functions.
- After all edits, run `tsc --noEmit` in `/Users/hube/Code/Udmy/TomPhillips/linkshortenerprj` to confirm no TypeScript errors.

Return ONLY: `subAgentSuccess: true` if all edits applied and TypeScript compiles, or `subAgentSuccess: false` followed by a brief explanation if it failed.

**S02: userId trusted from client** - Looking at actions.ts, the `userId` IS sourced from `auth()` on the server (line ~40: `const { userId } = await auth()`). The server actions DO NOT accept userId from the client. So S02 is actually already fixed/not a real issue in this codebase. But wait, let me look at the URL validation...


**S04: No URL validation/sanitization** - The actions.ts uses Zod's `.url()` validation, but this doesn't prevent `javascript:` URIs. Zod's URL constructor might allow these in certain environments, so I need to add explicit scheme validation to block dangerous protocols.

**S03: No rate limiting** - The createLinkAction needs rate limiting added to prevent abuse.

**S05: No interstitial warning** - An interstitial page or warning should appear before the redirect happens.

Now I'm working through the fixes. For S01, the redirect route just passes through whatever URL is stored in the database, so fixing S04's validation will help, but I should also add a scheme check directly in the redirect route as a defense-in-depth measure. For S02, I'm verifying that userId is always sourced from `auth()` in the server actions and never accepted as a client parameter—this is actually implemented correctly, so it's a false positive for this codebase, though I should still address it in the security review.

My plan is to add scheme validation to both the Zod schemas in actions.ts and the redirect route itself, implement an interstitial page for S05, confirm S02 is already secure, and add rate limiting for S03.

For rate limiting, since the project doesn't have Redis or external services configured beyond Clerk and Postgres, I'll implement a simpler approach using per-user rate limiting rather than installing additional dependencies.

The cleanest solution is to query the database and count how many links the user has created in the last hour, then enforce a limit there. This keeps everything within the existing infrastructure and works reliably in a serverless environment.

Now I need to coordinate dispatching subagents for each of the five issues. S02 doesn't actually need a code change since the userId verification is already correct. But S01, S04, and S05 all touch the same files, so running them in parallel could cause conflicts. Let me think through the dependencies and order them appropriately.

Since actions.ts needs both the URL validation (S04) and rate limiting (S03) changes, and route.ts needs both the scheme check (S01) and interstitial page (S05) changes, I should handle them sequentially by file. I'll start with S04 to add URL scheme validation to the Zod schemas...

Now I'm setting up database-based rate limiting by adding a function that counts recent links created by a specific user within a given timeframe, then integrating this check into the actions...

Actually, let me verify that the links table schema includes a `createdAt` field before proceeding.

For the interstitial warning page, I need to modify the redirect route to point users to an intermediate page that warns them before they're sent to the final destination.

The tricky part is passing the destination URL safely—I could use a query parameter, but that opens up potential open redirect vulnerabilities. A better approach would be to have the route redirect to `/redirect` and let the interstitial page fetch the actual destination URL using the short code, which keeps the URL validation server-side.

Actually, I think the cleanest solution is to redirect from `app/l/[shortCode]/route.ts` to `/go/[shortCode]` instead, then create an interstitial page there that fetches the destination from the database and displays a confirmation before redirecting. children.text: description: Read this before implementing any data mutations (create, update, delete) in the project.
All data mutations must be done via **Server Actions**. No API routes or client-side fetch calls may be used for mutations.
- Server actions must be called from **Client Components** only.
- Server action files **must be named `actions.ts`** and colocated in the same directory as the component that calls them.
- **Never use the `FormData` TypeScript type** — all data passed to server actions must use explicit TypeScript types.
- All input data **must be validated with Zod** before any business logic or database operations.
- Every server action **must verify a logged-in user** (via `auth()` from Clerk) before proceeding. Never trust a `userId` from the caller.
- Database operations must be done via **helper functions in the `/data` directory**. Server actions must not import or call Drizzle queries directly.
- **Never throw errors** from server actions. Always return a typed object with either a `success` or `error` property.
type ActionResult = { success: true } | { error: string };
description: Read this before implementing or modifying authentication in the project.
All authentication in this app is handled exclusively by **Clerk**. No other auth method, custom session logic, JWT handling, or password logic may be used.
- Never implement custom auth, sessions, or token logic.
- Never trust a `userId` from a request body — always source it from `auth()` on the server.
- `/dashboard` is a protected route. Unauthenticated users must not be able to access it.
- Use `clerkMiddleware` in `proxy.ts` to enforce this. Example:
- If a logged-in user visits `/`, redirect them to `/dashboard`.
- Implement this in the homepage Server Component using `auth()` and `redirect()`:
## Sign In / Sign Up — Modal Only
- Sign-in and sign-up must always open as a **modal**, never navigate to a dedicated page.
- Use `<SignInButton mode="modal">` and `<SignUpButton mode="modal">` for all trigger buttons.
- Do not create `/sign-in` or `/sign-up` route pages.
